steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Command of Evidence question asking which finding would most directly challenge the second team's conclusion. The second team concluded that Kozak and Keoleian (K&K) had better-explained methodology than Chancerel and Rotter (C&R). The passage ties "better-explained methodology" to traceability: a higher traceability rating means clearer descriptions of procedures. To challenge the conclusion, you need a finding that directly contradicts it.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — The study by Chancerel and Rutter had a medium consistency rating and a medium traceability rating.: This gives C&R's ratings in isolation but makes no comparison to K&K, so it does not challenge the conclusion that K&K's methodology was better explained. ✗
  • Choice B — The study by Kozak and Keoleian had a lower consistency rating than the study by Chancerel and Rotter did.: Consistency rating is not the same as traceability, and "better-explained methodology" is defined through traceability. A lower consistency rating does not directly undermine the conclusion. ✗
  • Choice C — The study by Kozak and Keoleian had a high consistency rating and a high traceability rating.: High ratings would support the conclusion, not challenge it. ✗
  • Choice D — The study by Kozak and Keoleian had a lower traceability rating than the study by Chancerel and Rotter did.: Traceability directly measures how clearly procedures are described. If K&K's traceability is lower than C&R's, then K&K's methodology was less clearly explained — the opposite of the second team's conclusion. ✓
D is correct.
